region_of_origin: Global (commonly produced in USA, Europe, and Asia)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of dried plums is approximately 5 pieces (about 40 g), commonly consumed as a snack or added to oatmeal, yogurt, or baked goods.

nutrient_source.title: Dried plums (prunes), uncooked — HPB Singapore Foodo / USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 09291)
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) and USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Primary values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo (cr_id F04030023, confidence 0.95). Cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for dried plums (prunes), uncooked (FDC ID 168162) for saturated fat and additional micronutrient data. Values are consistent between sources.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 29 (low) for prunes (dried plums) is directly reported in Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283), referenced in the University of Sydney GI Database. GL per serving calculated as: GI × available carbohydrate per serving / 100 = 29 × (63.88 × 0.40) / 100 = 29 × 25.55 / 100 ≈ 7.4. Serving size used: 40 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (38.1 g/100 g) despite very low sodium (2 mg/100 g) and high potassium (732 mg/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: While dried plums are very low in sodium and rich in potassium — both beneficial for blood pressure — their high sugar content (38.1 g/100 g) can drive insulin-mediated sodium retention; limit intake to 3–4 prunes per sitting (~30–40 g) and avoid combining with other high-sugar foods.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High sugar content (38.1 g/100 g) exceeding the 20 g/100 g glycaemic ceiling, despite low GI of 29 due to sorbitol
type2_diabetes.guidance: Dried plums carry a low GI (29) owing to sorbitol content, but the concentrated sugar density (38.1 g/100 g) poses a significant glycaemic risk at typical snack portions; individuals with type 2 diabetes should limit intake to 2–3 prunes (~20–25 g) per serving and pair with protein or fat to blunt postprandial glucose rise.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High potassium (732 mg/100 g), high dietary fibre (7.1 g/100 g), and polyphenol content offset by high sugar load
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Dried plums provide cardioprotective potassium (732 mg/100 g), soluble fibre (7.1 g/100 g), and polyphenols (neochlorogenic acid) that support vascular health, but the high sugar content (38.1 g/100 g) limits recommended intake to small portions (~30–40 g/day) to avoid triglyceride elevation and excess caloric intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sugar content (38.1 g/100 g) exceeds the 15 g/100 g RA pro-inflammatory threshold despite polyphenol content
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Dried plums contain anti-inflammatory polyphenols and fibre, but the high sugar concentration (38.1 g/100 g) promotes pro-inflammatory mediators; limit to 2–3 prunes per serving and pair with anti-inflammatory foods such as walnuts or fatty fish.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Dried plum, commonly known as prune, is a dehydrated form of the European plum (Prunus domestica), pitted and dried to produce a dark purple-black, wrinkled fruit with a chewy, sticky texture and a concentrated sweet-tart flavour. The drying process removes approximately 70% of the fruit's water content, intensifying its natural sugars (38.1 g/100 g), dietary fibre (7.1 g/100 g), and micronutrients including potassium (732 mg/100 g), calcium (43 mg/100 g), and iron (0.93 mg/100 g). Dried plums are widely consumed as a portable snack, added to breakfast cereals, oatmeal, trail mixes, baked goods, and savoury stews. They are well-known for their laxative effect, attributed to their high sorbitol content and soluble fibre, which promote bowel regularity. Despite a low glycemic index of 29 — due to sorbitol slowing glucose absorption — the high sugar density means portion control is essential for individuals managing type 2 diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. Dried plums are plant-based, gluten-free, halal, and kosher, making them broadly suitable across dietary preferences. They are a good source of polyphenols, particularly neochlorogenic and chlorogenic acids, which have demonstrated antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties in peer-reviewed research.
common_user_queries: How many prunes should I eat per day?
Are dried plums high in sugar?
What is the glycemic index of prunes?
Are prunes good for blood pressure?

Can I eat prunes if I have hypertension?

confidence_reason: Primary nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo (cr_id F04030023, confidence 0.95, reviewed status). Cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 168162) for dried plums (prunes), uncooked. Values are consistent between both approved sources. GI value of 29 is directly reported in Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care), referenced in the University of Sydney GI Database.
notes: Saturated fat (0.09 g), monounsaturated fat (0.05 g), and polyunsaturated fat (0.18 g) values sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 168162) as HPB record did not report fatty acid breakdown; values are consistent with the total fat of 0.38 g/100 g. Trans fat is 0.0 g — confirmed negligible per USDA SR Legacy. Vitamin B12 is 0.0 g and vitamin D is 0.0 mcg — confirmed zero as dried plums are a plant-based food with no animal-derived components; these are naturally zero values. Cholesterol is 0.0 mg — naturally zero as this is a plant-based food. The dietary tag 'wholegrain' present in the input has been removed as dried plums are not a grain product. GI of 29 is based on the sorbitol content of prunes, which slows intestinal glucose absorption; this is a well-established finding in the peer-reviewed literature. GL per serving (40 g) = 29 × 25.55 / 100 ≈ 7.4 (low GL). monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g supplemented from USDA SR Legacy as HPB source did not report these fields.
